---
title: "libinsight – Function Reference and Implementation Notes"
author: "Sahl Mohammed"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: rmarkdown::html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, eval = FALSE)
```

## Overview

This document contains information about the functions implemented in the
package libinsight, the reason for designing each function in the way it is,
and their relationship to one another. This document is stored in the `man/`
directory along with other documentation files in `.Rd` format.

---

## S3 Class: `libdata`

### What it does

`libdata()` wraps a plain data frame in an S3 class so that every other
function in the package can accept either a raw data frame or a `libdata`
object without duplicating validation logic.

```r
ld <- libdata(circ_sample)
class(ld)  # "libdata"
```

### Why S3 instead of S4

S3 was chosen because it is lightweight and sufficient for this use case.
The class only needs to carry one piece of state (the validated data frame)
and dispatch three methods. S4 would add overhead without benefit here.

### Methods

| Method | Behavior |
|---|---|
| `print.libdata` | Shows row count, unique items, subjects, patron types, year range, total checkouts |
| `summary.libdata` | Returns and prints monthly stats, subject counts, patron counts |
| `plot.libdata` | Bar chart of total checkouts grouped by subject |

---

## Function 1: `summarize_circulation()`

Computes total, mean, median, min, and max checkouts across monthly aggregates,
plus the calendar month and year with peak usage.

```r
summarize_circulation(ld)
summarize_circulation(ld, year_from = 2020, year_to = 2022)
```

**Implementation note:** The monthly aggregation calculations have been done
using `tapply()`, not `dplyr::summarize()`, since the summary itself should be
independent of any long chain; it is only arithmetic operations on groups
within a vector.

---

## Function 2: `plot_circ_trend()`

Draws a line chart of checkout volume over time. The `by` argument switches
between month-level and year-level aggregation.

```r
plot_circ_trend(ld)              # monthly
plot_circ_trend(ld, by = "year") # annual
```

**Implementation note:** In case of using `"month"` as the value of the
variable `by`, the values for the x-axis will be represented by a fraction
of the year (`year + (month - 1) / 12`).

---

## Function 3: `top_items()`

Returns the top-N items ranked by total checkouts. Optional `subject` and
`patron_type` arguments filter before ranking, which lets you answer questions
like "what do Graduate students check out most?"

```r
top_items(ld, n = 10)
top_items(ld, n = 5, subject = "Technology")
top_items(ld, n = 5, patron_type = "Graduate")
```

---

## Function 4: `patron_stats()`

Groups checkouts by patron type, computes totals and percentage share, and
plots a horizontal bar chart with percentage labels.

```r
patron_stats(ld)
patron_stats(ld, plot = FALSE)  # data only
```

**Implementation note:** The `plot` argument defaults to `TRUE` so the chart
appears automatically in interactive use, but can be suppressed when calling
`patron_stats()` from inside `lib_report()` to avoid duplicate output.

---

## Function 5: `collection_age()`

Draws a histogram of publication years weighted by checkout count. Items that
circulate more contribute more to the histogram, so the result reflects actual
usage patterns rather than just how many titles from each year are held.

```r
collection_age(ld)
collection_age(ld, binwidth = 10, fill = "#984EA3")
```

A dashed vertical line marks the checkout-weighted median publication year.

---

## Function 6: `lib_heatmap()`

Produces a tile heatmap with months on the x-axis and patron types on the
y-axis. Color intensity encodes total checkouts, making it easy to spot
which patron groups drive demand in specific months.

```r
lib_heatmap(ld)
lib_heatmap(ld, low = "#f7fbff", high = "#08306b")
```

---

## Function 7: `lib_report()`

Calls `summarize_circulation()`, `patron_stats()`, and `top_items()` in
sequence and formats their output as a plain-text console report with a
title and timestamp. Useful for quick one-call reporting.

```r
lib_report(ld, title = "Annual Report 2022", top_n = 10)
```

---

## Sample Dataset: `circ_sample`

`circ_sample` is an artificial dataset with 1,200 observations for 20 books,
6 subjects, 4 user categories, and 5 borrowing years (from 2018 to 2022).
Borrowing numbers are adjusted by a seasonal factor so that academic semesters
(January to May, September to December) have increased borrowing frequency —
creating a more interesting heatmap and trend plot using sample data.

The raw file can be found at `inst/extdata/circ_sample.csv`. The `data-raw`
directory holds the R code for creating the `.rda` file.

---

## Dependencies

| Package | Why it is used |
|---|---|
| `ggplot2` | All visualizations (trend, heatmap, histogram, bar charts) |
| `dplyr` | Grouped aggregation inside plot and stat functions |
| `stats` | `median()` in `summarize_circulation()` and `collection_age()` |
| `utils` | `head()` in `top_items()` |
